subject: ap-precalculus
title: Rational Functions: Covariation, Asymptotic Behavior & Model Validity
stimulus: A car dealership models the resale value of a used vehicle using the function $V$ given by $V(x) = \frac{28}{1 + 0.4x}$ for $x \geq 0$, where $V(x)$ is the value in thousands of dollars and $x$ is the number of years since purchase. The function $M$ gives the cumulative maintenance cost of the car, in thousands of dollars, as a function of $x$, and is given by $M(x) = 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^x$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Find the average rate of change of $V$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$, and from $x = 2$ to $x = 4$. Express answers in thousands of dollars per year. Show supporting work.

(ii) Based on the two average rates of change found in part (i), determine whether the average rate of change of $V$ is increasing or decreasing as $x$ increases from $0$ to $4$. Describe what this tells you about the concavity of the graph of $V$ on this interval, and interpret what this means in the context of car depreciation.
parts.scoring_notes: Exact values: $V(0) = 28$, $V(2) = 140/9 \approx 15.556$, $V(4) = 280/26 = 140/13 \approx 10.769$. Rate on $[0,2]$: $(140/9 - 28)/2 = (140/9 - 252/9)/2 = (-112/9)/2 = -56/9 \approx -6.222$. Rate on $[2,4]$: $(140/13 - 140/9)/4$; common denominator 117: $(1260 - 1820)/117 ÷ 4... easier numerically: $\approx -2.394$. The key insight for part (ii) is that a rate going from $-6.22$ to $-2.39$ is increasing (becoming less negative), which corresponds to concave up. In context, depreciation slows over time — a key feature of rational depreciation models.

scoring_points.description: Both average rates of change correct — $V(0) = \frac{28}{1} = 28$, $V(2) = \frac{28}{1.8} \approx 15.556$, $V(4) = \frac{28}{2.6} \approx 10.769$; rate on $[0,2]$: $\frac{15.556 - 28}{2} = \frac{-12.444}{2} \approx -6.222$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-6.23, -6.22]$); rate on $[2,4]$: $\frac{10.769 - 15.556}{2} = \frac{-4.787}{2} \approx -2.394$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-2.40, -2.39]$); both values required with supporting work

required_elements: Both average rates of change correct — $V(0) = \frac{28}{1} = 28$, $V(2) = \frac{28}{1.8} \approx 15.556$, $V(4) = \frac{28}{2.6} \approx 10.769$; rate on $[0,2]$: $\frac{15.556 - 28}{2} = \frac{-12.444}{2} \approx -6.222$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-6.23, -6.22]$); rate on $[2,4]$: $\frac{10.769 - 15.556}{2} = \frac{-4.787}{2} \approx -2.394$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-2.40, -2.39]$); both values required with supporting work
alternatives.correct_example: Both average rates of change correct — $V(0) = \frac{28}{1} = 28$, $V(2) = \frac{28}{1.8} \approx 15.556$, $V(4) = \frac{28}{2.6} \approx 10.769$; rate on $[0,2]$: $\frac{15.556 - 28}{2} = \frac{-12.444}{2} \approx -6.222$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-6.23, -6.22]$); rate on $[2,4]$: $\frac{10.769 - 15.556}{2} = \frac{-4.787}{2} \approx -2.394$ thousand dollars/year (accept $[-2.40, -2.39]$); both values required with supporting work

scoring_points.description: Correct conclusion with full interpretation — the average rate of change goes from $\approx -6.22$ to $\approx -2.39$, which is an increase (becoming less negative); the average rate of change is increasing, so the graph of $V$ is concave up on $[0, 4]$; in context: the car is still losing value (rate is negative) but the rate of depreciation is slowing down — the car loses value more slowly as it ages; all three elements required: (1) rate is increasing, (2) concave up, (3) contextual meaning about depreciation slowing

parts.prompt:
(i) Determine the end behavior of $V(x)$ as $x$ increases without bound. Express your answer using the notation of a limit, and interpret it in the context of car value.

(ii) Find the value of $x$ at which the cumulative maintenance cost $M(x)$ equals the car's resale value $V(x)$. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show or describe the method used, and explain what this value of $x$ represents for a car owner.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), as $x \to \infty$, the denominator $1 + 0.4x \to \infty$, so $V(x) = 28/(1+0.4x) \to 0$. The limit notation must include all four components. The contextual note that value approaches but never reaches zero (due to the horizontal asymptote at $y = 0$) is an important insight. For part (ii), both functions are in thousands of dollars, so no unit conversion is needed. The equation $28/(1+0.4x) = 0.15(1.2)^x$ is transcendental and requires technology. A graphing calculator intersection near $x \approx 8.0$ is expected. The interpretation must connect the crossover point to a practical car-ownership decision.

scoring_points.description: Correct limit $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$ with all four limit notation components ('lim', '$x \to \infty$', function $V$, value $0$); AND contextual interpretation: over a very long time, the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ (worthless) but never actually reaches zero — both the mathematical result and the contextual meaning required

required_elements: Correct limit $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$ with all four limit notation components ('lim', '$x \to \infty$', function $V$, value $0$); AND contextual interpretation: over a very long time, the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ (worthless) but never actually reaches zero — both the mathematical result and the contextual meaning required
alternatives.correct_example: Correct limit $\lim_{x \to \infty} V(x) = 0$ with all four limit notation components ('lim', '$x \to \infty$', function $V$, value $0$); AND contextual interpretation: over a very long time, the car's resale value approaches $\$0$ (worthless) but never actually reaches zero — both the mathematical result and the contextual meaning required

scoring_points.description: Correct crossover value $x \approx 8.0$ years (from $\frac{28}{1 + 0.4x} = 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^x$; graphing both sides or using technology yields intersection near $x \approx 8.0$; accept values in $[7.8, 8.2]$); AND interpretation: at approximately $x \approx 8$ years, the annual maintenance cost equals the car's resale value — beyond this point, the car costs as much to maintain as it is worth, suggesting the owner may consider replacing it

parts.prompt:
A financial analyst proposes an alternative model for the car's total cost of ownership, $T(x) = V(x) + M(x)$, defined as the sum of the car's current resale value and cumulative maintenance cost.

(i) Find the value of $T(3)$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Using technology, determine the approximate value of $x$ at which $T(x)$ achieves its minimum on $0 \leq x \leq 15$. Interpret the meaning of this minimum value in the context of car ownership.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $V(3) = 28/2.2 \approx 12.727$ and $M(3) = 0.15 \times 1.728 = 0.259$, so $T(3) \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars. For part (ii), $T(x)$ decreases initially (because $V$ drops steeply while $M$ grows slowly) then increases (as $M$ grows exponentially and $V$ flattens near zero). The minimum occurs near $x \approx 8$ years. The interpretation must connect the minimum to a meaningful car-ownership statement — e.g., the combined cost is lowest around 8 years.

scoring_points.description: Correct value — $V(3) = \frac{28}{1 + 1.2} = \frac{28}{2.2} \approx 12.727$ thousand dollars; $M(3) = 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^3 = 0.15 \cdot 1.728 = 0.259$ thousand dollars; $T(3) = 12.727 + 0.259 \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars (accept values in $[12.97, 13.00]$); supporting work must show both $V(3)$ and $M(3)$ evaluated separately before adding

required_elements: Correct value — $V(3) = \frac{28}{1 + 1.2} = \frac{28}{2.2} \approx 12.727$ thousand dollars; $M(3) = 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^3 = 0.15 \cdot 1.728 = 0.259$ thousand dollars; $T(3) = 12.727 + 0.259 \approx 12.986$ thousand dollars (accept values in $[12.97, 13.00]$); supporting work must show both $V(3)$ and $M(3)$ evaluated separately before adding

scoring_points.description: Correct minimum location — using technology, $T(x) = \frac{28}{1+0.4x} + 0.15 \cdot (1.2)^x$ achieves a minimum near $x \approx 8.0$ to $8.5$ years on $[0, 15]$ (accept values in $[7.5, 9.0]$); AND interpretation: this minimum represents the year at which the total combined cost (resale value plus maintenance) is lowest, suggesting the optimal time to sell or evaluate the car's financial worth
